coredata.dc:title: Biomass-derived carbon dots with light conversion and nutrient provisioning capabilities facilitate plant photosynthesis
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Bingxu
Yang, Zhilin
Chen, Feiran
Yue, Le
Cao, Xuesong
Li, Jing
Qian, Hai-Long
Yan, Xiu-Ping
Wang, Chuanxi
Wang, Zhenyu
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs)-enabled agriculture has been developing rapidly, but small-scale synthesis and high costs hinder the agricultural application of CDs. Herein, biomass-derived carbon dots (B-CDs) were prepared on a gram-level with low cost, and these B-CDs significantly improved crop photosynthesis. The B-CDs, exhibiting small size and blue fluorescence, were absorbed by crops and enhanced photosynthesis via light-harvesting. Foliar application of B-CDs (10 mg·kg−1) could promote chlorophyll synthesis (30–100 %), Ferredoxin (Fd, 40–80 %), Rubisco enzyme (20–110 %) and upregulated gene expression (20–70 %), resulting in higher net photosynthetic rates (130–300 %), dry biomass (160–300 %) and fresh biomass (80–150 %). Further, the B-CDs could increase crop photosynthesis under nutrient deficient conditions, which was attributed to the release of nutrients from B-CDs. Therefore, the B-CDs enhanced the photosynthesis via enhancing light conversion and nutrient supply. This study provides a promising material capable of enhancing photosynthesis for sustainable agriculture production.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass-derived carbon dots
Light-harvesting
Crop photosynthesis
Light conversion
Sustainable agriculture

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effects of B-CDs on photosynthetic activity of isolated chloroplasts
Crop growth and foliar exposure to B-CDs
The light conversion function of B-CDs
Enhanced photosynthetic system by B-CDs in Brassica chinensis L. leaves
Photosynthesis and growth of Brassica chinensis L. promoted by B-CDs
Growth of lettuce promoted by B-CDs under nutrient deficiencies

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 42077296 , 41820104009 , and 42192572 ), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( JUSRP622019 and JUSRP221023 ), Suzhou University of Science and Technology ( XTCXSZ2022-12 ), and Agricultural Science and Technology Innovation Project of Jiangsu Province ( CX (21)3073 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of facile process for fabricating B-CDs (A); High resolution TEM images of B-CDs prepared from Soybean, Walnut, and Laver (B–D); UV absorption, excitation and emission spectra of B-CDs prepared from Soybean, Walnut, and Laver (E–G).

The high-resolution XPS spectra of various B-CDs. A, XPS spectra of B-CDs prepared from soybean; B, XPS spectra of B-CDs prepared from walnut; C, XPS spectra of B-CDs prepared from laver. D-F, the C 1s of B-CDs prepared from soybean (D), walnut (E), and laver (F); G–I, the O 1s of B-CDs prepared from soybean (G), walnut (H), and laver (I); J–L, the N 1s of B-CDs prepared from soybean (J), walnut (K), and laver (L).

The light conversion function of B-CDs. SEM images and mapping of the chloroplast and CDs (CA and B-CDs) modified chloroplasts (A); overlapping between the absorption of chloroplasts and PL emission of B-CDs (B); DCPIP reduction rates of the chloroplast and CDs (CA and B-CDs) modified chloroplasts (C); the fluorescence life of Soy-CDs and its modified chloroplasts (D); UV–Vis absorption spectra of chloroplast conjugated with different B-CDs (E).

The uptake of B-CDs by Brassica chinensis L. leaves and enhanced photosynthetic system of Brassica chinensis L. leaves by B-CDs. CLSM images of the Brassica chinensis L. leaves by foliar application of Soy-CDs (A); chlorophyll fluorescence parameters (NPQ, QY, and Rfd) of plants under CA and various B-CDs treatments (B).

The promoted photosynthesis and growth of Brassica chinensis L. by treated B-CDs. Chlorophyll content (A); ferredoxin content (B); relative expression of genes psaA and Bna02806220, which related to chlorophyll synthesis and photosynthetic system activity (C and D); Rubisco enzyme activity (E); net photosynthetic rate (F); carbohydrate content (G); fresh and dry weights of plants after exposing CA and various B-CDs (H and I).

Release of Mg, Cu, and Fe from B-CDs solution. Mg (A), Cu (B), Fe (C). Response of chlorophyll, electron transfer rate, and ferredoxin content in plant leaves after exposure to B-CDs under different elements (Mg, Cu, and Fe) deficiency conditions. Chlorophyll content (D), electron transfer rate (ETR) (E), ferredoxin (Fd) (F).

head.ce:title: Biomass-derived carbon dots with light conversion and nutrient provisioning capabilities facilitate plant photosynthesis

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs)-enabled agriculture has been developing rapidly, but small-scale synthesis and high costs hinder the agricultural application of CDs. Herein, biomass-derived carbon dots (B-CDs) were prepared on a gram-level with low cost, and these B-CDs significantly improved crop photosynthesis. The B-CDs, exhibiting small size and blue fluorescence, were absorbed by crops and enhanced photosynthesis via light-harvesting. Foliar application of B-CDs (10 mg·kg−1) could promote chlorophyll synthesis (30–100 %), Ferredoxin (Fd, 40–80 %), Rubisco enzyme (20–110 %) and upregulated gene expression (20–70 %), resulting in higher net photosynthetic rates (130–300 %), dry biomass (160–300 %) and fresh biomass (80–150 %). Further, the B-CDs could increase crop photosynthesis under nutrient deficient conditions, which was attributed to the release of nutrients from B-CDs. Therefore, the B-CDs enhanced the photosynthesis via enhancing light conversion and nutrient supply. This study provides a promising material capable of enhancing photosynthesis for sustainable agriculture production.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Population growth and food safety are the main global challenges (El Bilali et al., 2018). According to food and agriculture organization of the united nations (FAO) statistics, food demand will be doubled to feed the rapidly growing population, which will reach 9.8 billion by 2050 (Timsina, 2018). Photosynthesis converts chemical energy into bioenergy and increase accumulation of biomass (Bailey-Serres et al., 2019). Enhancing photosynthesis is an effective way to promote crop production (Bailey-Serres et al., 2019; Yao et al., 2017). In addition, fertilizers (enriched with macronutrients and micronutrients) have made outstanding contributions to increase food production, leading to the agricultural revolution (Brownlie et al., 2021). By 2050, fertilizer demand is expected to increase to 263 Mt (Kah et al., 2018), but the use efficiency of fertilizers was <20 %, resulting in serious environmental issues (e.g., soil erosion, desertification, and secondary salinization) (You et al., 2022). Therefore, new technologies and materials are urgently needed to increase food production through regulation of photosynthesis and nutrient supply.

Recently, engineered nanomaterials (ENMs)-enabled agriculture have developed rapidly that can reduce nutrient loss and minimize the deterioration of the environment (Gilbertson et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2022a). Among these, carbon dots (CDs) showed promising in improving plant growth, resistance, and quality by their smaller size, superior fluorescence properties and biocompatibility (Gong and Zhao, 2018; Guo et al., 2022a). CDs can not only promote seed germination and increase water uptake by upregulating the expression of aquaporin-related genes (Park et al., 2014), but also alleviate abiotic stress in plants by regulating carbohydrate metabolism, rhizosphere process and nutrient assimilation (Yang et al., 2022). CDs can be used as a kind of nanofertilizer to promote crop growth and yield (Guo et al., 2022b). In addition, CDs are good light-harvesting materials in improving the photosynthetic efficiency of crops (Wang et al., 2021a). For example, CDs can convert ultraviolet light into visible light or infrared light, thereby increasing the range of light utilization by plants and promoting photosynthesis (Li et al., 2020; Li et al., 2018). CDs also improved photosynthesis by increasing the activity of Rubisco, thereby increasing crop yields (Li et al., 2019). However, small-scale synthesis and high cost hinder the agricultural application of CDs. Besides, although CDs could promote utilization of nutrients and light (Wang et al., 2022b), it remains unclear whether CDs have a light-nutrition coupling effect to improve crop photosynthesis.

The advantages of using natural resources for CDs synthesis are cost-effective and eco-friendly (Zhang and Yu, 2016). The green synthesis methods are highly acceptable as compared with physical and chemical processes (Das et al., 2018). Therefore, biomass-derived CDs (B-CDs) from natural carbon sources offer a feasible approach to achieve large-scale synthesis of CDs with low cost (Ayiloor Rajesh et al., 2022). Besides, waste organic products are used for the preparation of CDs (Hsu et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2015). For instances, 1 g of coffee grounds were used as raw material to prepare 120 mg of CDs (Hsu et al., 2012). 10 g of bee pollens as a carbon source can produce >3 g of CDs (Zhang et al., 2015). Moreover, the nutrients contained in B-CDs favor the regulation of plant physiological processes (Li et al., 2021). Therefore, we hypothesized that B-CDs could promote plant photosynthesis by enhancing light conversion and nutrient supply. The aims of this study were to investigate 1) the large scale (gram-level) synthesis method and characterization of B-CDs; 2) the absorption of B-CDs enhances the net photosynthetic rate in plant leaves; 3) the light-nutrition coupling effect of B-CDs for plant nutrition supply.

B-CDs were synthesized through hydrothermal synthesis using soybeans, walnuts， and dried laver as precursors, respectively. Eight grams of soybeans were ground into powder and dissolved in 30 mL ultrapure water. Then the mixture was transferred to a 50 mL PTFE-lined stainless-steel autoclave and further maintained at 200 °C for 6 h. The reactants were sequentially filtered through 4 layers of gauze and 0.22 μm filter membrane to remove biomass-based macroaggregates. Finally, soybean B-CDs (Soy-CDs) were obtained by dialysis (MW 500) against ultrapure water for 48 h and freeze-drying. Walnuts B-CDs (Wal-CDs) and laver B-CDs (Lav-CDs) were synthesized in a similar way, and CDs (citric acid CDs, CA) prepared from citrate acid were synthesized as previously described (Wang et al., 2015). The morphology and size of B-CDs and CA were investigated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Talos F200X G2, Thermo-Fisher, USA, operating at an acceleration voltage of 200 kV). The elemental components of B-CDs were determined by X–ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-Alpha, Thermo-Fisher, USA) and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP–MS, iCAP–TQ, Thermo-Fisher, USA). The UV–Vis spectra and fluorescence spectra of B-CDs were obtained by ultraviolet spectrophotometer (UV–1800, Shimadzu, JAP) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (F–1800, Hitachi, Japan). The fluorescence decay curve of B-CDs was determined by transient/steady-state fluorescence spectrometer (FLS–1000, Edinburgh, UK). The functional groups of B-CDs were determined by Fourier Infrared spectrometer (FTIR, NEXUS, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of B-CDs on photosynthetic activity of isolated chloroplasts
ce:section.ce:para: Chloroplast suspension was obtained according to a previous report (Wang et al., 2021b). 5 mL of chloroplast suspension and B-CDs (10 mg·kg−1, 10 mL) were uniformly mixed in sucrose solution for 10 min, then centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 3 min to obtain a complex of B-CDs and chloroplasts (B-CDs/Chl). Finally, B-CDs/Chl suspension was re-dispersed in sucrose buffer. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, SU8010, HITACHI, Japan) was used to observe the morphology of B-CDs/Chl suspension. The fluorescence images and energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) mapping were used to determine the distribution of B-CDs on the surface of chloroplasts. Meanwhile, the UV–vis spectrum, fluorescence emission spectrum of chloroplasts, and fluorescence decay curve of B-CDs/Chl were recorded. The effect of B-CDs on chloroplast photosynthetic activity was studied using Hill reaction. The mixture of the B-CDs/Chl suspension and 2, 6-dichlorophenol indophenol (DCPIP) (final concentration of 60 μM) is irradiated immediately under room temperature and at 1000 lx. The reduction rate of DCPIP was determined according to the absorption peak at 600 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Crop growth and foliar exposure to B-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Pakchoi seeds (Brassica chinensis L. Suzhou Qing No. 9) were purchased from Jiangsu Academy of Agricultural Sciences, China. Seeds were soaked with 1 % sodium hypochlorite for 10 min, rinsed 3 times with deionized water, and kept in a dark environment. The seedlings were transplanted into square pots (10 cm × 10 cm, containing 1 kg of soil) and grown in a greenhouse with a temperature of 20/15 °C (day/night), with a photoperiod of 18/6 h (light/dark) and a maximum light intensity of 15,000 lx. No additional fertilizer was applied throughout the growing season. After three weeks, 10 mg·L−1 of different types of B-CDs and CDs were sprayed at 5 mL per plant every time for 7 consecutive days, while the control group (CK) was sprayed with deionized water. After foliar application of B-CDs, photosynthetic parameters including net photosynthetic rate (Pn), intercellular CO2 content (Ci), stomatal conductance (Gs), and transpiration rate (Tr) were measured by the CERAS–3 portable gas exchange system of Hansatech. The photosystem activities (ETR, QY_Lss, NPQ, and Rfd_Lss) were analyzed using photon systems instruments (FlourCam FC800, PSI, Czech). The root parameters were measured by a Canadian WinRHIZO Pro 2017b root sweeper. The uptake and distribution of B-CDs were determined by confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM, Nikon, A1, Japan, excitation wavelength of 405 nm) and TEM (JEOL, Japan). The element content, chlorophyll content, Ferredoxin content, gene expression level, Rubisco enzyme activity, and carbohydrates were determined according to previous studies (Wang et al., 2022c; Wang et al., 2022d).

The B-CDs solution (10 mg·L−1, 50 mL) was dialyzed against deionized water through a dialysis membrane (MW 500) for 48 h. During this period, the dialysate was collected with a time gradient of 6, 12, 18, 24, 36, 36, 42, and 48 h. The elements content in dialysates were analyzed using inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometry (ICP–MS, iCAP–TQ, Thermo-Fisher, USA).

In the nutrient deficiency test, hydroponic culture of lettuce (Lactuca sativa L.) was spiked with Hoagland Basal Salt mixture deficient in Fe, Mg, and Cu elements. Briefly, the seeds were washed and germinated in a dark environment for 3 days, and seedling of uniform growth were transplanted into a clean hydroponic pot (2 L) with 3 plants in each pot. 7 treatments were set including CK, deficiency of Mg (D-Mg), Cu (D-Cu), and Fe (D-Fe), and after application of the corresponding B-CDs (Soy-CDs, Wal-CDs, and Lav-CDs). CK was treated with Hoagland Basal Salt mixture without any deficiencies. The nutrient solution was changed every 3 days. Symptoms plants deficient in Mg, Cu, Fe, as well as the effects after B-CDs application were recorded.

All treatments were performed in at least three replicates. All data were presented as average value ± standard deviation (SD). One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with Fisher LSD test (p < 0.05) was used to analyze the differences between different treatments group.

The biomass-derived carbon dots (B-CDs) were synthesized by hydrothermal method using soybeans, walnuts, and lavers as raw materials, respectively (Fig. 1A). Three B-CDs showed similar size distribution (6–20 nm) and average size (~10 nm) as shown in Figs. 1B–D and S1. Moreover, all B-CDs had absorption bands at 263 nm and 320–380 nm, owing to the π-π* transition of the CC bond and n-π* transitions respectively (Fig. 1E–G) (Wang et al., 2015). In addition, the three B-CDs showed similar blue fluorescence with an optimal emission wavelength of 430 nm at excitation wavelength located near 350 nm (Fig. 1E–G). The excitation-dependent fluorescence was also observed (Fig. S2) due to uneven surface chemistry and various size distributions, which was similar as most reported CDs (Wang et al., 2016). Then, the FT–IR spectra showed the broad absorption band at 1027, 1265, 1581, 1702, 2930 and 3250 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of CO, CN, CC, CH and CO, respectively (Fig. S3A-C) (Mintz et al., 2021). XPS spectra showed that all B-CDs contained C, N, and O elements (Fig. 2A–C) (Mintz et al., 2021). The high-resolution C 1s spectra demonstrated the existence of CC (284.2 eV), CN (285.6 eV), CO (286.7 eV) (Fig. 2D–F) (Mintz et al., 2021). The O 1s spectra involves two peaks attributed to CO (533.7 eV) and CO (531.9 eV) (Fig. 2G–I) (Mintz et al., 2021). The N 1s spectra exhibited two peaks that were assigned to pyrrole N (400 eV) and graphite N (401.3 eV) (Fig. 2J–L) (Mintz et al., 2021). These results indicated that prepared B-CDs had good hydrophilic surface, which allowed them to have a good distribution in aqueous solution (Fig. 1A).

As-prepared B-CDs were different in that they contained various nutrients (Mg, Cu, and Fe) doping (Figs. 2J–l and S4), which may correspond to the element (Mg, Cu, and Fe) content of raw material itself (Ayiloor Rajesh et al., 2022; Das et al., 2018; Zhang and Yu, 2016). For example, B-CDs prepared from soybean contained 10,539.3 ± 638.4 mg·kg−1 of Mg, and those prepared from walnut contained 15.9 ± 1.9 mg·kg−1 of Cu and prepared from laver contained 361.9 ± 16.2 mg·kg−1 of Fe. XPS spectra showed that Soy-CDs, Wal-CDs, and Lav-CDs contained Mg, Cu, and Fe elements respectively (Fig. S4). These results suggested that the composition of B-CDs depended on raw biomass itself (Ayiloor Rajesh et al., 2022; Das et al., 2018; Zhang and Yu, 2016), which may facilitate the design and preparation of B-CDs with specific structures and functions. Moreover, natural waste biomass could offer a large and economical carbon source for the preparation of CDs-based materials (Sharma et al., 2017), contributing to resource utilization of solid waste as well as the low cost and large-scale synthesis of functional CDs (Hsu et al., 2012; Zhang et al., 2015). Herein, through optimizing the reaction conditions (e.g., raw material dosage, reaction temperature and reaction time), the maximum yield of B-CDs could reach up to 50 % (Table S2), and g-level synthesis of B-CDs could be easily achieved. Meanwhile, the cost for the raw materials was very low (about 32.6 ￥·kg−1, B-CDs prepared from soybeans) (Table S3). Therefore, this study provided a solution for the low cost and large-scale synthesis of B-CDs, implying great potential in sustainable agriculture production.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The light conversion function of B-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The blue fluorescence of Soy-CDs distributed around chloroplast implied the coating of Soy-CDs on chloroplast membrane (Fig. S5). This result was further confirmed by SEM images (Fig. 3A). The surface of chloroplast remained unchanged after coating with B-CDs as compared to CK (Fig. 3A). Moreover, some elements (e.g., C, N and O) could be detected on the surface of both un-exposed and modified chloroplasts. Distinctively, the Mg, Cu and Fe were detected in the chloroplasts modified by B-CDs (Fig. 3A). The contents of Mg, Cu, Fe in chloroplasts were significantly increased by modification with B-CDs (Fig. S6). These results illustrated that B-CDs were successfully compounded on the surface of chloroplasts. Previous studies demonstrated that fluorescent ENMs could enhance the photosynthetic function of chloroplasts due to their unique light harvesting ability (Huang et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2017). Particularly, blue fluorescent CDs have been proven to be used as light conversion materials to enhance photosynthesis (Li et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2021a). The blue fluorescence of prepared B-CDs could overlap with the absorption of chloroplasts, suggesting that the prepared B-CDs, like most reported CDs (Wang et al., 2021a), could be used as light conversion materials to improve photosynthesis.

Compared with the unmodified chloroplasts, the emission spectra of the three modified chloroplasts had a larger overlap area with that of chloroplast, indicating that both B-CDs and CDs (citric acid CDs, CA) improved the light capture and photosynthesis (Fig. 3B). In line with this, the Hill reaction was designed by using DCPIP, whose reduction rate could represent electrons transport from PS II to PS I during the photoreaction of chloroplasts (Wang et al., 2021b). The results demonstrated the reduction rate of DCPIP was significantly accelerated after application of B-CDs and CA (Fig. 3C). Specifically, the B-CDs prepared from soybean showed maximum reduction rate of 155.5 %, while the others (Wal-CDs and Lav-CDs) showed 104.9 % and 51.1 %, respectively, which were higher than that of CA (3.6 %) (Fig. 3C). These results indicated that B-CDs outperformed CA in improving plant photosynthetic activity.

Meanwhile, the fluorescence life of modified chloroplasts by Soy-CDs decreased from 3.74 to 2.38 ns, compared with Soy-CDs alone (Fig. 3D), indicating that the energy of B-CDs can be absorbed and utilized by chloroplasts under UV illumination. Comparing with the absorption curves of chloroplast, the peak curve of chloroplasts modified with B-CDs were significantly higher, indicating that B-CDs improved the light absorption intensity of chloroplast and promote the light absorption (Fig. 3E). Therefore, B-CDs promoted the absorption and conversion of light by chloroplasts, leading to enhanced photosynthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced photosynthetic system by B-CDs in Brassica chinensis L. leaves
ce:section.ce:para: Previous studies demonstrated that the uptake and translocation of fluorescent ENMs could be visualized by imaging techniques (CLSM, TEM, and HRTEM (high resolution transmission electron microscopy)) (Li et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2021b). In this study, CLSM images showed strong blue fluorescence of Soy-CDs at an excitation wavelength of 405 nm (Fig. 4A). On the contrary, no fluorescence appeared in CK (Fig. 4A), since lower autofluorescence signals of CLSM testing parameters were set due to the endogenous blue-greenish fluorescence in most plant tissues. Plants leaves absorb ENMs through the epidermal pathway and leaf stomata, which could be affected by many factors including size and surface chemistry (Lv et al., 2019). For example, CDs could enter the roots, stems, leaves of mung bean plant due to their small size (Li et al., 2016). Meanwhile, CDs with the tensile vibration of hydrophilic groups (e.g., OH and NH) could be observed in various plants (Li et al., 2017). Therefore, we supposed that B-CDs could enter plant leaves due to their small size and surface hydrophilicity. Furthermore, TEM and HRTEM images of leaves collected from Brassica chinensis L. exposed to Soy-CDs supported these results. As shown in Fig. S7, typical morphology of Soy-CDs with lattice spacing of 0.21 nm was clearly observed around chloroplast. Therefore, B-CDs could penetrate the leaves cells and be transported to organelles, which is important for the development of plant nanobionics to alter or amplify the function of the plant tissues or organelles (Giraldo et al., 2014; Wu et al., 2017).

Previous reports demonstrated that ENMs in leaves could amplify photosynthetic function (Giraldo et al., 2014). Herein, the fluorescent intensity of NPQ (representing the light retention capacity), Rfd (representing the potential viability) and QY (representing the photochemical efficiency) were stronger in Brassica chinensis L. leaves after exposure to B-CDs compared with CK and CDs (Fig. 4B), indicating a higher photosynthetic ability. The parameters NPQ and Rfd in plants can reflect the photosynthetic efficiency of plants (Zhang et al., 2016), while QY represents the speed of electron transfer efficiency in the photosynthetic system (Wu et al., 2021). Under exposure to B-CDs, the NPQ was increased by 31.1 %, 1.3 %, and 11.9 %, Rfd was increased by 52.6 %, 32.7 %, and 44.2 %, and QY was increased by 30 %, 76.7 %, and 0 %, respectively (Fig. S8A). Moreover, B-CDs could also improve other photosynthetic parameters, including qP, qN, and ETR. Soy-CDs, Wal-CDs, and Lav-CDs showed increases in qP (photochemical quenching) by 3.1 %, 25.0 %, and 22.9 %; qN (non-photochemical quenching) by 9.2 %, 1.3 %, and 6.6 %; and ETR (electron transfer rate) by 68.0 %, 112.0 %, and 20.0 %, respectively (Fig. S8A). Other related parameters such as NPQ-L1, qN-L1, qP-L1 were also increased after B-CDs exposure (Fig. S8B). These results demonstrated that B-CDs were conducive to promote the efficient operation and coordinated transmission of photosynthetic electrons in the photosystem, and affect the plant vitality by adjusting the efficiency of photosynthesis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photosynthesis and growth of Brassica chinensis L. promoted by B-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In in vivo experiments, B-CDs could improve many photosynthetic system indicators, including chlorophyll, ferredoxin (Fd), Rubisco enzyme, and genes (Fig. 5 ). As an important pigment for photosynthesis, chlorophyll converts light energy into chemical energy in photosynthesis (Sun et al., 2021). Fd is a small molecule protein with electron transporter effects (Morales et al., 2000). The Soy-CDs, Wal-CDs, and Lav-CDs resulted in significant increases in chlorophyll a by 103.6 %, 94.6 %, and 81.8 %; chlorophyll b by 36.1 %, 36.0 %, and 16.4 %, respectively (Fig. 5A); in addition, Fd content in leaves were significantly improved by 65.1 %, 44.4 %, and 85.8 %, respectively (Fig. 5B). The relative expression of genes (psaA and Bna02806220) related to photosystem activity and chlorophyll synthesis were significantly up-regulated (photosystem activity, 39.9 %, 16.5 %, 21.2 %; chlorophyll synthesis, 76.2 %, 54.4 %, 48.8 %) (Fig. 5C and D). Rubisco enzyme is a key enzyme in photosynthesis that determine the rate of carbon assimilation and plant photorespiration (Li et al., 2019). The Soy-CDs, Wal-CDs, and Lav-CDs improved Rubisco enzyme by 68.0 %, 112.0 %, and 20.0 %, respectively (Fig. 5E). Besides, compared with CK, the net photosynthetic rates were significantly improved by 350.0 %, 153.8 % and 138.5 %, respectively (Fig. 5F), and similar trend were observed for other parameters (e.g., Ci, Tr, and Gs) (Fig. S9). Previous studies demonstrated that CDs could use light conversion capacity to improve plant absorption and utilization of light, thereby increasing the photosynthetic rate and activity (Li et al., 2020; Li et al., 2019; Li et al., 2018). Therefore, B-CDs can improve photosynthesis of plants by enhancing light conversion.

Photosynthesis plays a vital role in producing organic matter for plant growth (Bailey-Serres et al., 2019; Yao et al., 2017). Carbohydrates are important products of photosynthesis, providing substances and energy for the growth and development of plants (Wang et al., 2021c). After exposing Soy-CDs, Wal-CDs, and Lav-CDs, the carbohydrate accumulation increased by 41.8 %, 38.6 %, and 28.6 % (Fig. 5G), respectively. Moreover, compared with CK, the fresh weight of shoots was increased by 311.0 %, 241.9 %, and 164.5 %, respectively; the fresh weight of roots was increased by 145.8 %, 139.6 %, and 131.3 %, respectively (Fig. 5H); the dry weight of shoot was increased by 152.0 %, 108.6 %, 82.2 % and root increased 194.4 %, 150.8 %, 135.6 % (Fig. 5I). B-CDs could also increase the root surface area, root length, and average root diameter of plants (Fig. S10). Therefore, foliar application of B-CDs significantly improved the photosynthetic system and promoted carbohydrate accumulation, leading to an increase in crop biomass.

In addition, B-CDs made from different materials regulate the photosynthesis in different ways. For example, Soy-CDs mainly facilitated the synthesis of chlorophyll; Wal-CDs accelerated the electron transport rate in photosynthetic system; while Lav-CDs increased the accumulation of ferritin in plants. This could be due to the fact that the raw materials for the preparation of B-CDs from soybean, walnut, and laver are rich in Mg, Cu, and Fe, which can be doped into the B-CDs during the synthesis process and may be released over time. Mg is an essential element in the composition of the pigment (chlorophyll a) (Li et al., 2021). Cu participates in electron transport and photosynthetic phosphorylation, thus affecting the rate of electron transport (Hunter et al., 2022). Approximately 75 % of Fe in plant cells binds to chloroplasts, lipoproteins in chloroplasts, and mitochondrial membranes (Salome et al., 2013). Ferritin, which is stored in plant leaves, is used to form plastids and is required for photosynthesis (Kroh and Pilon, 2020). Therefore, various doped B-CDs could regulate the process of photosynthesis in different ways to improve the photosynthetic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Growth of lettuce promoted by B-CDs under nutrient deficiencies
ce:section.ce:para: The release rates of Mg, Cu, and Fe from Soy-CDs, Wal-CDs, Lav-CDs were 2.5 %, 0.45 %, and 6.0 % (Fig. 6A–C) respectively, indicating that B-CDs could supply nutrients for crop growth. Mg is a component of chlorophyll, and the typical symptoms of Mg deficiency (D-Mg) are losing green (Silva et al., 2021), even resulting in leaves drying and abscission (Fig. S11A). The symptoms of Cu deficiency (D-Cu) in plants were manifested as poor growth, deformed leaves and losing green (Fig. S11B) (Vatansever et al., 2017). Iron deficiency (D-Fe) leads to uniform yellowing of leaves (Vatansever et al., 2017), the junction of the diseased part is not obvious, and the yellowing of the top leaf is particularly serious (Fig. S11C). As shown in Fig. S10, foliar application of B-CDs could maintain lettuce growth under deficiency state. Specifically, fresh weight of shoots increased by 8.9 %, 60.9 %, and 56.2 %, respectively, and that of roots increased by 64.6 %, 68.5 %, and 37.9 %, respectively (Fig. S12A). Dry weight of shoots improved by 46 %, 65.8 %, and 35.8 %, respectively, while that of roots improved by 32.7 %, 37.7 %, and 17.8 % (Fig. S12B), respectively. Moreover, B-CDs also enhanced the carbohydrate content by 26.0 %, 34.7 %, and 19.0 % (Fig. S12C). Nutrients such as Mg, Cu, and Fe in plants were significantly improved under B-CDs treatment (Fig. S13) due to their nutrient delivery function. These nutrients significantly raised chlorophyll content by 78.3 %, 58.3 %, and 55.2 % (Fig. 6D), ETR by 12.9 %, 18.5 %, and 53.5 % (Fig. 6E), and Fd by 8.9 %, 0.6 %, and 21.3 % (Fig. 6F). These results indicated that B-CDs have nutrient delivery function that facilitate plant photosynthesis and maintains plant growth in nutrients deficient condition. Therefore, B-CDs may provide specific functional of nano-fertilizers to promote crop growth in different nutrient deficient situations.

Herein, a gram level and low-cost synthesis of CDs were achieved through using raw biomass. These various biomass-derived CDs (B-CDs) showed blue fluorescence, small size, rich hydrophilic surface, and good distribution in aqueous solution. Moreover, some nutrients (e.g., Mg, Cu, and Fe) from specific natural biomass were found in the structure of B-CDs, which could also be released from B-CDs. Thus, fluorescence- and nutrients-riched B-CDs have two functions: light conversion and nutrient supply. The results demonstrated that B-CDs could penetrate the leaves cells and be transported to organelles, leading to improved photosynthetic parameters (e.g., chlorophyll synthesis, Fd, Rubisco enzyme, and gene expression) due to high light conversion capacity and nutrient supply. Thus, foliar application of B-CDs significantly promoted carbohydrate accumulation and crop growth. In addition, B-CDs promoted crop growth in nutrient deficient situations due via increasing the supply of specific nutrients. Therefore, these B-CDs showed promising applications in sustainable agriculture production and global food security.

Bingxu Cheng: Writing-review & editing, Data analysis, Formal analysis. Zhilin Yang: Investigation, Data analysis. Le Yue: Investigation, Data analysis. Jing Li: Writing-review & editing. Feiran Chen: Investigation, Data analysis. Xuesong Cao: Investigation, Data analysis. Hailong Qian: Writing-review & editing. Xiuping Yan: Writing-review & editing. Chuanxi Wang: Investigation, Writing-original draft, Data analysis. Zhenyu Wang: Writing-review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (42077296, 41820104009, and 42192572), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (JUSRP622019 and JUSRP221023), Suzhou University of Science and Technology (XTCXSZ2022-12), and Agricultural Science and Technology Innovation Project of Jiangsu Province (CX (21)3073).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.scitotenv.2023.165973.
